content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Administration 
322-5660 
Ms. Cheryl Goble 
Correspondence Supervisor 
Governor's Office 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Ms. Goble: 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
July 21, 1981 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
This letter is sent in response to your letter dated 
July 10, 1981, requesting advice from this office as to 
whether you should amend your 1980 Statement of Economic 
Interest filed pursuant to the requirements of the Political 
Reform Act. 
This adviI7 is provided pursuant to Government 
Code Section 83114(b).-
My understanding of the facts is as follows: 
You re-
ceived a check for $200 made out to you from Californians 
for Brown on November 28, 1980. 
You stated that "[tJhe 
funds were provided for the purchase of California state 
flags, as needed, when requested by California servicemen 
and veterans groups." 
After cashing the check on November 
28, 1980, you gave the money to the Accounting Officer in 
the Governor's Office for placement in the Governor's Office 
safe. 
Since that date, the money sealed in an envelope has 
remained in the safe. 
None of the money has been withdrawn 
to purchase flags or for any other purpose. 
It was not 
intended that you use the money for personal purposes, and 
the money has never been used or available for use for your 
own benefit. 
In essence, you were the conduit through which 
the Committee cashed a check. 
The issue raised by the facts you provided is whether 
this event resulted in income to you within the meaning of 
the political Reform Act which would be reportable on your 
Statement of Economic Interest. 
l/ All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise noted. 


Ms. Cheryl Goble 
July 21, 1981 
Page Two 
In this regard, Section 82030 of the Act provides in 
relevant part: 
a) 
"Income" means, except as provided in subsection 
(b), a payment received, including but not limited to 
any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, 
proceeds from any sale, gift ••• 
(Emphasis added) 
under the circumstances you described, although there was a 
check issued in your name, you did not receive the money 
within the meaning of Section 82030. 
You were not the in-
tended or the actual beneficiary of the money. 
Therefore, 
the $200 amount of the check is not reportable income under 
the Act, and you need not amend your Statement of Economic 
Interest. 
In any event, if you received less than $250 income 
($25 for a gift) from a single source during 1980, it is not 
reportable. 
only sources of income from which you received 
$250 or more during the period covered are reportable on 
your Statement of Economic Interest. 
Section 87207. 
If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to 
contact me at (916)322-5901. 
DMF:kp 
ve~y ~ours, 
( 
_ 
. 
4~/CJ ~~ 
Diane Maur 
Fishburn 
Counsel 
Legal Division

sections.question: Whether you should amend your 1980 Statement of Economic Interest filed pursuant to the requirements of the Political Reform Act based on receipt of a $200 check made out to you from Californians for Brown on November 28, 1980, where the funds were provided for the purchase of California state flags as needed by California servicemen and veterans groups, you cashed the check and gave the money to the Accounting Officer in the Governor's Office for placement in the Governor's Office safe where it has remained sealed and unused.
sections.conclusion: Under the circumstances you described, although there was a check issued in your name, you did not receive the money within the meaning of Section 82030. You were not the intended or the actual beneficiary of the money. Therefore, the $200 amount of the check is not reportable income under the Act, and you need not amend your Statement of Economic Interest. In any event, if you received less than $250 income ($25 for a gift) from a single source during 1980, it is not reportable.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a $200 check received by the requester and cashed on behalf of a political committee, but immediately placed in the Governor's Office safe and never used for personal benefit, constitute reportable income under the Political Reform Act's Statement of Economic Interest requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The $200 check is not reportable income because the requester was merely a conduit for the political committee and was neither the intended nor actual beneficiary of the funds. Additionally, the amount falls below the $250 reporting threshold for income from a single source.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document discusses whether a check cashed by the requester but held in trust for political purposes constitutes reportable income. Footnote reference to Government Code Section 83114(b) as authority for advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether you should amend your 1980 Statement of Economic Interest filed pursuant to the requirements of the Political Reform Act based on receipt of a $200 check made out to you from Californians for Brown on November 28, 1980, where the funds were provided for the purchase of California state flags as needed by California servicemen and veterans groups, you cashed the check and gave the money to the Accounting Officer in the Governor's Office for placement in the Governor's Office safe where it has remained sealed and unused.

CONCLUSION: Under the circumstances you described, although there was a check issued in your name, you did not receive the money within the meaning of Section 82030. You were not the intended or the actual beneficiary of the money. Therefore, the $200 amount of the check is not reportable income under the Act, and you need not amend your Statement of Economic Interest. In any event, if you received less than $250 income ($25 for a gift) from a single source during 1980, it is not reportable.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Ms. Cheryl Goble that she need not amend her 1980 Statement of Economic Interest regarding a $200 check received from Californians for Brown, as she was only a conduit for the funds and did not personally benefit from them, thus the amount is not reportable income under the Political Reform Act.
